title: Cell Wall Inhibitors
summary: مثبطات جدار الخلية البكتيرية تشمل مجموعتين رئيسيتين: البيتا-لاكتام (Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Monobactams) والببتيدات السكرية (Glycopeptides). آلية عمل البيتا-لاكتام هي الارتباط ببروتين PBP الذي يثبط تشابك جدار الخلية مما يؤدي إلى انتفاخها وانفجارها. الفانكومايسين (Vancomycin) يثبط تفاعل التوليد السكري ويُستخدم خصيصاً ضد MRSA والجراثيم الموجبة لغرام. مقاومة البنسلين تحدث عبر إنتاج بيتا-لاكتاماز أو تغيير PBP أو زيادة مضخة الطرد.

key_drugs.name: Penicillin G
key_drugs.note: IV، ضيق الطيف، فعّال ضد G+ والمكورات وسبيروكيتات

key_drugs.name: Ampicillin
key_drugs.note: IV، امتصاص فموي ضعيف، طيف موسّع

key_drugs.name: Imipenem/Cilastatin
key_drugs.note: آخر خط دفاع، يُعطى مع cilastatin لمنع تحلله

key_drugs.name: Aztreonam
key_drugs.note: G- فقط، آمن في حساسية البيتا-لاكتام

key_drugs.name: Vancomycin
key_drugs.note: MRSA، تحذير Red Man Syndrome، سمية كلوية وسمعية

key_drugs.name: Benzathine Penicillin G
key_drugs.note: عضلي، مفعول 3-4 أسابيع، وقاية الحمى الرثوية

mnemonics.title: LAME+C: ما لا تغطيه السيفالوسبورينات
mnemonics.body:
السيفالوسبورينات لا تفعل شيئاً مع LAME+C:
L = Listeria monocytogenes
A = Atypicals (Chlamydia, Mycoplasma)
M = MRSA
E = Enterococci
C = Clostridium difficile
تذكّر: السيف يقطع كل شيء ما عدا الـ LAME!

mnemonics.title: جيل الكيفالوسبورين = طابق في البناية
mnemonics.body:
تخيّل بناية من 4 طوابق:
- الطابق الأول (1st gen): ناس خليجيين G+ فقط، لا يخرجون (لا يعبرون BBB)
- الطابق الثاني (2nd gen): ناس خليجيين وعرب G+/G-، لا يخرجون
- الطابق الثالث (3rd gen): يفتحون النوافذ ويصلون الدماغ (يعبرون BBB)
- الطابق الرابع (4th gen - Cefepime): يفتحون كل الأبواب + Pseudomonas

mnemonics.title: Red Man Syndrome - الرجل الأحمر
mnemonics.body:
تخيّل حقن الفانكومايسين بسرعة كبيرة، فيخرج الهيستامين من خلاياه الصارية مثل بركان!
وجه المريض ورقبته وجذعه تصبح حمراء كالطماطم.
الحل: أعطِ الفانكومايسين ببطء على مدى 60 دقيقة على الأقل.

mnemonics.title: MICE: مقاومة البنسلين المكتسبة
mnemonics.body:
M = Methicillin resistance (MRSA) - تغيير PBP
I = Impermeability (تغيير الـ porin - نقص نفاذية)
C = Cephalosporinase / Beta-lactamase production
E = Efflux pump increase
البكتيريا تقاوم البنسلين بـ MICE!

mnemonics.title: Aztreonam - الوحيد الآمن في عالم الحساسية
mnemonics.body:
أزترونام (Aztreonam) = أَز + تري + النام = 'الذي نام بمفرده'.
يعمل وحده ضد G- فقط، ولا يتشارك الحساسية مع بقية البيتا-لاكتام.
إذا كان المريض يعاني من حساسية لكل البيتا-لاكتام، Aztreonam هو المخرج للعدوى بـ G-.

mnemonics.title: ضد Pseudomonas: CAMPFIRE
mnemonics.body: Carbapenems, Aminoglycosides, Monobactams, Polymyxins, Fluoroquinolones, thIRd & fourth gen cephalosporins, Extended-spectrum penicillins.

mnemonics.title: ممنوعة بالحمل: SAFE Moms Take Really Good Care
mnemonics.body: Sulfonamides (kernicterus), Aminoglycosides (ototoxicity), Fluoroquinolones (cartilage damage), Erythromycin estolate (cholestatic hepatitis), Metronidazole (mutagenesis), Tetracyclines (teeth/bone), Ribavirin (teratogen), Griseofulvin (malformations), Chloramphenicol (grey baby syndrome).

mnemonics.title: آمنة بالفشل الكلوي: DANCER
mnemonics.body: Doxycycline, Azithromycin, Nafcillin (monitor), Ceftriaxone/Cefoperazone, Erythromycin, Rifampicin — تُطرح أساساً عبر الكبد/الصفراء.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of penicillin?
flashcards.back: It binds to PBP (transpeptidase) inside the bacterial cell and inhibits peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bacterial swelling and lysis (bactericidal).

flashcards.front: Which penicillins are well absorbed orally?
flashcards.back: Penicillin V, Amoxicillin, Amoxicillin + clavulanic acid (Augmentin), Indanyl Carbenicillin.

flashcards.front: What is Probenecid and its role with penicillin?
flashcards.back: It inhibits the renal tubular secretion of penicillin, raising its blood levels and prolonging its effect.

flashcards.front: What is Augmentin?
flashcards.back: Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid - given orally; clavulanic acid inhibits beta-lactamase and protects the amoxicillin.

flashcards.front: When is Benzathine Penicillin G used?
flashcards.back: Rheumatic fever prophylaxis (IM every 3-4 weeks), endocarditis prophylaxis, and syphilis.

flashcards.front: What are the LAME+C organisms not covered by cephalosporins?
flashcards.back: Listeria, Atypicals (Chlamydia/Mycoplasma), MRSA, Enterococci, C. difficile.

flashcards.front: Which cephalosporin generation crosses the blood-brain barrier?
flashcards.back: The third and fourth generations (except Cefixime and Cefoperazone of the third generation).

flashcards.front: What is the T1/2 of Ceftriaxone and why is it notable?
flashcards.back: T1/2 = 4-7 hours (the longest among cephalosporins), so it is given once daily.

flashcards.front: Why is Imipenem given with Cilastatin?
flashcards.back: Because Imipenem is degraded by renal dehydropeptidase-I in the kidney; Cilastatin inhibits this enzyme and raises plasma levels.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Imipenem and Meropenem in toxicity?
flashcards.back: Imipenem causes seizures (CNS toxicity); Meropenem is less likely to cause seizures because it is not degraded by dehydropeptidase.

flashcards.front: Which organisms are covered by Aztreonam only?
flashcards.back: Gram-negative aerobic rods only: Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae, N. gonorrhoeae.

flashcards.front: Why is vancomycin not effective against Gram-negative bacteria?
flashcards.back: Its molecule is too large to penetrate the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria.

flashcards.front: What is Red Man Syndrome and how do we avoid it?
flashcards.back: Flushing of the face, neck, and trunk due to histamine release when vancomycin is infused rapidly. Prevention: administer it over 60 minutes or more.

flashcards.front: What is the main toxicity of vancomycin?
flashcards.back: Ototoxicity (auditory toxicity) + Nephrotoxicity (renal toxicity), which increase when combined with aminoglycosides.

flashcards.front: When is vancomycin used orally?
flashcards.back: Only in antibiotic-associated colitis (C. difficile colitis) - because it is not absorbed from the intestine.

flashcards.front: What is Bacitracin and its use?
flashcards.back: A glycopeptide that inhibits the cell wall; it is nephrotoxic if given systemically, so it is used topically only for skin infections.

flashcards.front: What are the adverse effects of penicillins?
flashcards.back: 1) IgE hypersensitivity reaction (anaphylactic shock) 2) Diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis (C. diff with Ampicillin) 3) Vitamin K deficiency 4) Platelet dysfunction (Piperacillin) 5) Seizures.

flashcards.front: Can a cephalosporin be given to a patient allergic to penicillin?
flashcards.back: If the penicillin allergy is a severe anaphylactic reaction, the cephalosporin should not be given. Cross-reactivity is 5-10%.

flashcards.front: What is the alternative to penicillin in case of allergy, for Gram-positive and Gram-negative infections?
flashcards.back: Gram-positive: Macrolides, Quinolones, or Vancomycin (severe infection). Gram-negative: Aztreonam.

flashcards.front: Which carbapenem is best for meningitis?
flashcards.back: Meropenem (crosses the BBB and has less CNS toxicity compared to Imipenem).

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of penicillins?
flashcards.back: They bind to PBP (transpeptidase) and block the cross-linking of peptidoglycan -> cell wall lysis -> bactericidal.

flashcards.front: Why are penicillins ineffective against fungi and viruses?
flashcards.back: Because they do not have a peptidoglycan wall (no target).

flashcards.front: What are the mechanisms of penicillin resistance?
flashcards.back: Production of β-lactamase, altered PBP (MRSA/VRSA), altered porin, and efflux pumps.

flashcards.front: What is the role of Clavulanic acid?
flashcards.back: A β-lactamase inhibitor added to amoxicillin (Augmentin) to restore its efficacy.

flashcards.front: What is the spectrum across successive cephalosporin generations?
flashcards.back: As the generation advances, Gram-negative coverage generally increases and Gram-positive coverage decreases.

flashcards.front: What is the use of Vancomycin?
flashcards.back: MRSA and serious Gram-positive infections; orally for C. difficile colitis.

flashcards.front: What is Red man syndrome?
flashcards.back: Flushing and rash from rapid vancomycin infusion (histamine release) - slow the infusion.

flashcards.front: What is Aztreonam?
flashcards.back: A monobactam active against aerobic Gram-negative bacteria only, safe with penicillin allergy.

flashcards.front: What are the carbapenems?
flashcards.back: Imipenem/Meropenem - very broad-spectrum, resistant to most β-lactamases.

mcqs.q: A pregnant patient needs an antibiotic to treat a bacterial infection. Which antibiotic is the safest?
mcqs.explanation: Penicillins are the safest antibiotics for pregnant women. They do not cause fetal malformations and cross the placenta safely.

mcqs.q: Which enzyme does Cilastatin inhibit when given with Imipenem?
mcqs.explanation: Cilastatin inhibits Renal dehydropeptidase-I, which degrades Imipenem in the kidney, thereby raising its plasma levels.

mcqs.q: A patient is infected with MRSA. Which antibiotic is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Vancomycin is the primary treatment for MRSA. Aztreonam is active against Gram-negative bacteria only, and beta-lactams are ineffective against MRSA.

mcqs.q: A patient with penicillin anaphylaxis has a Pseudomonas infection. Which is the most appropriate option?
mcqs.explanation: Aztreonam does not share cross-reactivity with the other beta-lactams, and it is active against Gram-negative bacteria including Pseudomonas.

mcqs.q: Which cephalosporin has the longest T1/2 and is given once daily?
mcqs.explanation: Ceftriaxone has a T1/2 of 4-7 hours, the longest among cephalosporins, so it is given once or twice daily.

mcqs.q: What is the mechanism of MRSA resistance to penicillin?
mcqs.explanation: MRSA is resistant due to an altered PBP (penicillin-binding protein), which prevents beta-lactams from binding to it.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is used topically only because of its severe nephrotoxicity?
mcqs.explanation: Bacitracin is highly nephrotoxic when used systemically, so it is used topically only to treat skin infections.

mcqs.q: A patient receiving rapid IV Vancomycin develops flushing of the face and neck. What is the diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Red Man Syndrome occurs from rapid vancomycin infusion, leading to histamine release. Treatment: slow the infusion.

mcqs.q: أي الكاربابينيم هو الأقل احتمالاً لإحداث تشنجات؟
mcqs.explanation: Meropenem أقل احتمالاً للتشنجات لأنه لا يتحلل بـ renal dehydropeptidase ولا يتراكم بكميات كبيرة.
Unasyn = Ampicillin + Sulbactam (IV/IM). أما Augmentin = Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid (فموي).
glycopeptide ضد موجبة الغرام بما فيها MRSA.

high_yield.point: الآلية: تثبيط PBP (transpeptidase) → تعطّل بناء الببتيدوغليكان → قاتلة للجراثيم (bactericidal).
فعّالة فقط على الجراثيم المنقسمة؛ غير فعّالة على الفطريات/الفيروسات/Mycoplasma (لا جدار).
المقاومة: إنتاج β-lactamase، تغيّر PBP (MRSA/VRSA)، تغيّر porin، مضخات الإفلاكس.
Vancomycin (glycopeptide) لـ MRSA؛ يُعطى IV للعدوى الجهازية؛ خطر 'Red man syndrome' بالحقن السريع.
